name: Stillwater
short_description: A Minnesota rivertown destination for rockhounding along the scenic St. Croix River, where glacial deposits and exposed bedrock offer opportunities to find quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens.
description: Stillwater sits along the St. Croix River in east-central Minnesota, a geologically rich area shaped by Pleistocene glaciation and underlying Precambrian bedrock. The region offers rockhounders access to glacial erratics, river deposits, and exposed outcrops along the riverbanks and nearby hillsides. Quartz in various forms, feldspar, and mica are the primary finds, with occasional jasper and chert from glacial transport. The town itself is charming and walkable, making this an excellent destination for families combining rockhounding with sightseeing. Most productive collecting occurs in the spring when winter runoff exposes fresh material along the river, and in fall when water levels drop. Access to prime rockhounding areas varies from easy riverside walks to moderate hikes up wooded hillsides with river views.
history: This area sits within the Superior Province of the Canadian Shield, where Precambrian granitic and metamorphic rocks were exposed and heavily glaciated during the Pleistocene. The St. Croix River valley was carved by glacial meltwater, leaving behind erratics and stream deposits of quartz, feldspar, and mica that rockhounds can find today along the riverbanks and in glacial drift deposits.
tips: Focus your search along the St. Croix River shoreline, especially where water has exposed gravel bars and rocky outcrops after seasonal water level changes. Quartz and feldspar specimens are most abundant in areas with exposed bedrock and in glacial drift deposits on hillsides overlooking the river. A small hand lens and digging tool help identify feldspar crystal faces and mica sheets. The best specimens often appear after heavy rain or spring snowmelt when the river churns and deposits fresh material along its banks.
terrain_notes: Elevation ranges from approximately 685 feet at river level to 800+ feet on surrounding hills. Terrain varies from flat to gently rolling woodland with some steeper bluffs overlooking the river. Walking distance from town parking to productive areas ranges from 0.25 to 2 miles. Footing can be uneven along the river shoreline with loose rocks, gravel bars, and rooted trail sections. Some areas require modest elevation gain on well-used but uneven paths.
best_season: April - October, with peak conditions in April - May (spring runoff and fresh exposures) and September - October (lower water levels revealing new material). Winter and early spring flooding can obscure access, while summer heat is mild compared to more southern states.
written_directions: From downtown Stillwater, head toward the St. Croix River at Main Street or Riverside Drive. Several public access points exist along the river corridor including Riverfront Park and the Lowell Park area. For Riverfront Park, use Main Street heading east toward the river; parking is available in the downtown area. For Lowell Park, follow County Road 5 north from downtown approximately 2 miles to park entrance on the left (east) side of the road. River access areas are generally within walking distance of parking.
road_conditions: All access roads are paved. County Road 5 is well-maintained. No high-clearance vehicle needed.
parking_notes: Public parking available at Riverfront Park (downtown) with multiple spaces and restroom facilities. Lowell Park has dedicated parking lot with capacity for 20+ vehicles. Street parking available in downtown Stillwater near river access points.
collection_rules: Collect on public lands and parks for personal use only. Check with Stillwater Parks and Recreation for any specific park collection policies. No commercial collection. Respect private property - stay on public access points and marked trails. Leave no trace and do not disturb vegetation or park facilities.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial activity
Stay on public access areas and trails
Respect park rules and posted signs
Take only what you can carry and use
Do not disturb vegetation or damage park infrastructure
nearest_services: Downtown Stillwater is the nearest town with full services including gas, restaurants, shops, and accommodations, located 0-2 miles from most rockhounding access points.
accessibility_notes: Riverfront Park area is relatively flat and accessible to most hikers, with paved paths to river overlooks. Some river shoreline access requires navigating loose rocks and uneven ground. Lowell Park area involves modest elevation changes and uneven woodland trails. Overall terrain is beginner-friendly with good footing in parks but rockier along the river itself. No wheelchair access to active rockhounding areas along shoreline.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Stillwater, Minnesota?
faq.answer: The primary finds at Stillwater are quartz (including clear crystals and milky varieties), feldspar with visible crystal faces, mica sheets, and jasper. These minerals come from both Precambrian bedrock exposures and glacial deposits left during the Pleistocene ice age.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free in Stillwater, Minnesota?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding is free at public access points along the St. Croix River and in parks like Riverfront Park and Lowell Park. No permits or fees are required for personal collection on public lands.

faq.question: What is the best time of year for rockhounding in Stillwater?
faq.answer: April through October offers the best conditions, with peak collecting in spring (April-May) when snowmelt exposes fresh material, and in fall (September-October) when lower water levels reveal new specimens along the river shoreline.

faq.question: Can beginners and families rockhound in Stillwater?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Stillwater is excellent for beginners and families. Riverfront Park offers easy, flat access to the river, and basic specimens of quartz, feldspar, and mica are abundant. No specialized tools or experience are required to get started.

faq.question: Do you need special tools or permits to collect rocks at Stillwater?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use collection. A small hand lens, digging tool, and collection bag are helpful but not essential. A basic hammer and chisel can aid specimen extraction from exposed bedrock.

faq.question: How close is Stillwater rockhounding to downtown, and are dogs allowed?
faq.answer: Most rockhounding access points are within 0-2 miles of downtown Stillwater, making it easy to combine collecting with shopping and dining. Dogs are generally welcome on public access trails and parks, though you should verify current park policies with Stillwater Parks and Recreation.

meta_description: Hunt quartz, feldspar, and mica along the scenic St. Croix River near Stillwater, Minnesota. Free public access, family-friendly, and beginner-perfect rockhounding.
